1.synthesis: Six days before Passover, a dinner is given for Jesus in Bethany. Martha serves; Lazarus reclines at table with him. Mary takes a pound of expensive nard, anoints Jesus's feet, and wipes them with her hair — and the house fills with fragrance (v.3). Judas Iscariot objects that the perfume could have been sold for three hundred denarii and the money given to the poor (vv.4–5). The Evangelist notes he was a thief who controlled the money box (v.6). Jesus answers: "Leave her alone, so that she may keep it for the day of my burial" (v.7), and adds the Deuteronomy saying: "The poor you always have with you, but you do not always have me" (v.8). Meanwhile a large crowd gathers — to see both Jesus and Lazarus — and the chief priests extend their plot to include killing Lazarus as well, since many Jews were believing in Jesus on account of him (vv.9–11).
1.historical_context: Nard (nardos pistike) was imported from the Himalayan foothills and was among the most expensive aromatics in the Mediterranean world; a Roman pound (twelve ounces) cost approximately three hundred denarii — a year's wages for a day-laborer. Mary's act reverses the expected honor code at every point: heads were anointed for royal or priestly distinction, not feet; loosing one's hair in a public setting was reserved for mourning or private intimacy in Jewish society. Judas's objection activates a real ethical framework — tsedaqah (charitable giving to the poor) was a primary Jewish religious obligation — making his argument sound principled even to the other disciples (cf. Matthew 26:8; Mark 14:4). Jesus's response invokes Deuteronomy 15:11 — "the poor will never cease from the land" — a verse whose full context commands perpetual generosity, not indifference, while distinguishing the ongoing obligation from a unique and unrepeatable moment. The decision to kill Lazarus alongside Jesus (v.10) is a political-evidentiary calculation: Lazarus himself was walking testimony that could not be countered by argument.
1.literary_structure: The anointing scene is positioned as the private counterpart to the public entry (vv.12–19): in one, extravagant love is offered in intimate household space; in the other, messianic acclaim is offered in the streets. The Evangelist has already introduced Mary as "she who had anointed the Lord and wiped his feet with her hair" (John 11:2) before narrating the act, signaling the scene is already part of the Gospel's interpretive architecture. Jesus's reframe — this is a burial anointing (v.7) — turns an act of devotion into a Passion anticipation: the nard spent here will not be needed on Easter morning (John 20:1–9) because the body that receives it will not remain in the tomb long enough to require anointing. Judas's exposure as a thief (v.6) in the same breath as his principled-sounding objection to "waste" frames the entire betrayal narrative: the one who steals the money will sell the man.
1.theological_application: Mary's act is the Gospel's model of costly, uncalculated worship. Against Judas's utilitarian calculus — what could this have accomplished? — stands the fragrance that fills the whole house. The extravagance of love for Christ is not a misallocation of resources; it is the recognition of what is most precious. "The poor you always have with you, but you do not always have me" (v.8) is not an excuse for neglecting the poor but a claim about the uniqueness of this moment and this person. Mary does not know the theology of what she is doing; Jesus interprets it as preparation for burial. That her act of devotion becomes a prophetic act without her knowing it is characteristic of the Gospel: those who simply come to Jesus in love find themselves caught up in purposes larger than they understood.
1.christology: The burial anointing frame places the Bethany dinner within the shadow of the cross. The nard poured on feet at a dinner table will prove to be the anointing that does not happen at the tomb, because the tomb will not hold the body long enough to need it. This is the Evangelist's first explicit placement of the cross-timeline on a domestic scene: six days before Passover, the one who will be crucified on its eve is at table with the one he raised. The contrast between Lazarus — raised, eating, potentially to be killed again — and Jesus — alive, eating, about to be killed and to rise — sets the terms for everything that follows: the raising of Lazarus is a sign; the resurrection of Jesus is the reality the sign points toward, and it will require no unbinding.

12.synthesis: The next day the Passover crowd hears that Jesus is coming to Jerusalem. They take palm branches and go out to meet him, crying "Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord, even the King of Israel!" (v.13). Jesus finds a young donkey and rides into the city, fulfilling: "Fear not, daughter of Zion; behold, your king is coming, sitting on a donkey's colt!" (v.15; cf. Zechariah 9:9). The disciples did not understand this at the time; they understood after Jesus was glorified (v.16). The crowd that had witnessed the raising of Lazarus testified to what they had seen, and many came out because they had heard of the sign (vv.17–18). The Pharisees, watching, say to each other: "Look, the world has gone after him" (v.19).
12.historical_context: Palm branches carried specific Maccabean resonance: Simon Maccabeus's entry into Jerusalem after the rededication of the Temple was celebrated with palm-waving (1 Macc 13:51; 2 Macc 10:7), embedding the gesture in the memory of Israel's last successful liberation from foreign oppression. "Hosanna" (hoshia-na, "Save us, please!") and "Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord" were the Hallel psalms (Psalms 118:25–26) sung during Passover; the crowd's addition of "the King of Israel" made the messianic application explicit. The Zechariah quotation Jesus enacts (Zechariah 9:9) describes a king who comes on a donkey's colt — the animal of peace, not the warhorse of military conquest — to speak peace to the nations and rule from sea to sea. The crowd receives a liberating king; Zechariah's king arrives as a peacemaker. The disciples' retrospective understanding — grasping the Scripture's meaning only "when Jesus was glorified" (v.16) — is the Evangelist's consistent epistemological pattern: events are comprehensible only in light of the resurrection.
12.literary_structure: The entry is framed by the Lazarus sign: the crowd that witnessed the raising (vv.17–18) generates the demonstration that brings the city out. The Pharisees' frustrated remark — "the whole world has gone after him" (v.19) — is immediately followed by the arrival of Greeks (vv.20–21), making the Pharisees' sarcasm into inadvertent prophecy. The disciples' non-understanding (v.16) is not a failure but a narrative marker: the entry cannot be understood before the cross because the kind of king Jesus is only becomes clear through his death. The Evangelist is constructing the entry as a scene that contains its own misreading: the crowd receives him as the liberating king they want; Zechariah's text describes the peaceful king they need.
12.theological_application: The entry is the Gospel's most public disclosure of Messianic identity, and it is immediately misunderstood by everyone present — crowd, disciples, and Pharisees alike. The crowd wants the Maccabean liberator; the disciples are caught up in the celebration without comprehension; the Pharisees see only a political threat. The Zechariah text, fulfilled without its own context being grasped, provides the corrective: this king comes "humble" and brings peace, not military dominion. The entry teaches that the categories through which people recognize and welcome Jesus are often the wrong ones, and that genuine understanding requires the interpretive event of the cross and resurrection. What the world called a parade, the Evangelist calls an approach to a throne made of wood.
12.christology: Jesus's choice of the donkey's colt is an acted interpretation of his own messianic identity: he deliberately fulfills Zechariah's sign of the peaceful king over and against the expectation of a military messiah. In doing so he claims the Messianic title while refusing the Messianic role his audience projects onto it. The Pharisees' complaint — "the whole world has gone after him" (v.19) — names the very mission statement of the Gospel: the Logos came into the world (John 1:9–10; John 3:16), and the world is the scope of his drawing. "Your king is coming" (v.15) is the entry's Christological announcement: the king who arrives on a borrowed donkey is the one through whom the Father will draw all people to himself when he is lifted up (v.32).

20.synthesis: Some Greeks at the festival approach Philip: "Sir, we wish to see Jesus" (v.21). The request is relayed through Andrew; Jesus answers with a statement that does not address the Greeks directly but announces the arrival of the hour: "The hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified" (v.23). A grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, and bears much fruit; the one who hates his life in this world will keep it for eternal life (vv.24–25). Jesus's soul is troubled — "save me from this hour? No — for this purpose I have come to this hour. Father, glorify your name" (vv.27–28). A voice from heaven answers: "I have glorified it, and I will glorify it again." The crowd hears thunder or an angel; Jesus says it was for their sake (vv.28–30). Now is the judgment of this world; the ruler of this world will be cast out; and "when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself" (v.32). The light is with them a little longer; believe in the light, become children of light (vv.35–36).
20.historical_context: The "Greeks" were almost certainly Gentile God-fearers — non-Jews attached to the synagogue through monotheism and ethical practice without full proselyte conversion — present at Passover as worshippers who could not enter beyond the Court of the Gentiles. Their request to "see" Jesus triggers the hour-announcement, making their arrival the signal of the universal mission's threshold moment. Philip and Andrew, both with Greek names, mediate the approach through the patron-client convention of layered brokerage. The Gethsemane-like troubled soul of v.27 is the closest the Fourth Gospel comes to the Synoptic agony in the garden (cf. Mark 14:34); in John, the petition for rescue is immediately retracted in favor of the Father's glorification. The divine voice (v.28) is the Johannine equivalent of the Synoptic Transfiguration voice; the crowd's divided hearing — thunder or an angel — reflects the two recognized categories of anomalous divine communication in Jewish tradition. "The ruler of this world will be cast out" (v.31) employs the Second Temple category of a hostile power exercising delegated authority over the present age — whose ejection at the cross is the Gospel's cosmic claim.
20.literary_structure: The Greeks' request occasions a disclosure that never directly answers them but reframes the question. They want to "see" Jesus; the seeing the Evangelist has in mind is the seeing that comes only through the cross: "when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself" (v.32). The grain-of-wheat saying (v.24) grounds the cosmic scope of the hour in a homely agricultural image: death is not loss but the condition of multiplication. The troubled soul and the immediate retraction (vv.27–28) show that the hour is entered fully — not with stoic indifference but with deliberate, costly consent. "Lifted up" (hypsoō, v.32) is the third of three such sayings in John (John 3:14; John 8:28), each holding crucifixion and exaltation in a single word. The closing light-urgency (vv.35–36) marks this as the end of the public ministry: after this, Jesus hides and is not found.
20.theological_application: "Unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit" (v.24) gives the cross its agrarian logic: what looks like loss is the condition of abundance. This principle is applied to discipleship in v.25: the life held tightly is the life that cannot grow; the life offered is the life that multiplies. The troubled soul of v.27 is not overcome by suppressing it but by turning it toward the Father's glorification: "for this purpose I have come to this hour." The hour that contains death also contains the judgment of the world's ruler and the drawing of all people — the cross is simultaneously the lowest point of humiliation and the highest disclosure of divine love.
20.christology: "When I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself" (v.32) is the third and climactic lifting-up saying in John. Each use of hypsoō holds together crucifixion and exaltation: the cross is the mechanism of his elevation above the earth, and from that elevation he draws all people. The arrival of Greeks seeking him is the first ripple of this drawing; the cross is its completion. The divine voice's answer — "I have glorified it, and I will glorify it again" (v.28) — confirms that the glorification is not lost in the cross but achieved through it: the past glorification (the signs) and the future glorification (the passion and resurrection) are a continuous act of the Father expressing himself through the Son.

37.synthesis: The Evangelist pauses to reflect on the failure of the signs to produce widespread belief: "Though he had done so many signs before them, they still did not believe in him" (v.37). He finds this predicted in Isaiah — "Lord, who has believed what he heard from us?" (Isaiah 53:1) — and in the blinding/hardening oracle of Isaiah 6:9–10: God himself had blinded their eyes and hardened their hearts, lest they turn and be healed (v.40). Isaiah saw Christ's glory and spoke of him (v.41). Nevertheless many even among the authorities believed, though they would not confess it for fear of expulsion — "they loved the glory that comes from man more than the glory that comes from God" (vv.42–43). Then Jesus's final public declaration: belief in him is belief in the one who sent him; seeing him is seeing the Father; he came as light; whoever hears his words and does not believe will be judged at the last day by those very words; the command of the Father is eternal life (vv.44–50).
37.historical_context: The Evangelist's double citation from Isaiah is the most concentrated prophetic interpretation in John. Isaiah 53:1 opens the fourth Servant Song with a question about failed reception; Isaiah 6:9–10 is the commission to speak a word that hardens as well as illumines — the most frequently cited OT text in the NT, appearing in all four Gospels and Acts. The Evangelist's version is the most direct of the NT citations: God blinds and hardens, where Matthew and the LXX are softer. The claim that Isaiah "saw his glory" (v.41) identifies the vision of YHWH enthroned in Isaiah 6:1 as a vision of the pre-incarnate Christ — a high Christological move equating the divine throne-room glory with the Son's pre-incarnate presence. "Secret believers" among the leaders (v.42) reflect the real social cost of confessing Jesus in a community where aposynagōgos (synagogue expulsion, John 9:22) was enforceable. The doxa-exchange — preferring human glory to God's glory (v.43) — is the Evangelist's diagnosis of the social mechanism that sustains unbelief across the Gospel (John 5:44).
37.literary_structure: The section closes the Book of Signs (John 1–12) and functions as the Evangelist's theodicy of rejected witness: why did the signs not produce faith? His answer layers prophetic prediction (Isaiah foresaw it), divine agency (God hardened), and human choice (they loved the wrong glory) without fully resolving the tension between them. The final public speech of Jesus (vv.44–50) has no narrative frame — the Evangelist does not say when or where Jesus said this — presenting it as a summary declaration that recapitulates the Gospel's central themes: belief in the Sent One is belief in the Sender; the light has come into the world; the words spoken will judge on the last day; the command is eternal life. These verses close the public ministry and prepare for the intimate Farewell Discourse of chapters 13–17.
37.theological_application: The coexistence of prophetic prediction and human responsibility in the unbelief diagnosis is one of the most demanding theological tensions in the Gospel. The Evangelist will not reduce it: both the divine blinding and the human love of human glory are offered as explanations for the same failure. The secret believers of vv.42–43 are presented not as heroes but as cautionary figures: genuine faith that will not pay the social cost of confession has not yet found its full form — it remains captive to the doxa of the social order rather than the doxa of God. The final speech presses the same choice: the words of Jesus are either life or judgment, and the criterion is whether one will come to the light.
37.christology: Isaiah's vision was of the Son's glory (v.41) — the pre-incarnate glory that the Prologue named before it was made visible in flesh (John 1:14). This claim identifies the OT's highest theophany as an appearance of the eternal Son, making the Incarnation not the beginning of the Son's story but its focal revelation. The summary Christology of vv.44–50 — belief in the Son as belief in the Father, seeing the Son as seeing the Father, the Son's words as the Father's command — ties together the entire public ministry's witness: every sign, every discourse, every claim has been the Father speaking through the Son for the purpose of eternal life. The Book of Signs ends with the purpose still open: "his commandment is eternal life" (v.50), and that life remains available to whoever will hear and come.
